title: C Syntax
content:
### C Syntax Rules

C syntax defines the structure and rules for writing C programs. Every C program follows a specific structure.

### Basic Structure

```
preprocessor directives

int main() {
  statements;
  return 0;
}
```

### Important Syntax Rules

| Rule | Description |
|------|-------------|
| Semicolons | Every statement ends with a semicolon `;` |
| Curly braces | Code blocks are enclosed in `{ }` |
| Case sensitivity | C is case-sensitive (`main` != `Main`) |
| Whitespace | Spaces, tabs, and newlines are ignored (except in strings) |
| Comments | `//` for single-line, `/* */` for multi-line |

---

### Keywords

C has 32 reserved keywords (C89) that cannot be used as identifiers:

`auto`, `break`, `case`, `char`, `const`, `continue`, `default`, `do`, `double`, `else`, `enum`, `extern`, `float`, `for`, `goto`, `if`, `int`, `long`, `register`, `return`, `short`, `signed`, `sizeof`, `static`, `struct`, `switch`, `typedef`, `union`, `unsigned`, `void`, `volatile`, `while`

---

> **Key Point:** Always terminate statements with `;` and use `{ }` to define function bodies and control structures.

quiz.question: How are statements terminated in C?
